coredata.dc:title: Extensive solar light utilizing by ternary C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3: Highly enhanced photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics and inactivation of E. coli
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanyuan
Li, Yue
Ruan, Zhaohui
Yuan, Yuan
Lin, Kaifeng
coredata.dc:description: Fabrication of a visible-light driven photocatalyst is of great vital for the elimination of antibiotics and microorganism in the wastewater and the construction of sustainable green energy systems. In this work, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) were integrated with Cu2O/SrTiO3 p-n heterojunction to optimize the photocatalytic activity. The excellent photocatalytic degradation efficiency of chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC·HCl) (92.6% within 90 min) and E. coli inactivation efficiency were observed over C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 under visible light irradiation. It is the synergistic effect of p-n heterojunction and modification of C-dots that facilitates the separation and transfer of electron-holes. Meanwhile, the modification of C-dots improves the harvesting of long wavelength solar light of photocatalysts due to its unique up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) characteristics. Eventually, the possible photocatalytic degradation path of the catalyst was inferred by LC-MS spectra, and the degradation mechanism was analyzed. This study sheds light on new possibilities for the application of photocatalysts in various light sources and has broad application prospects in water treatment.

head.ce:title: Extensive solar light utilizing by ternary C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3: Highly enhanced photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics and inactivation of E. coli

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of a visible-light driven photocatalyst is of great vital for the elimination of antibiotics and microorganism in the wastewater and the construction of sustainable green energy systems. In this work, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) were integrated with Cu2O/SrTiO3 p-n heterojunction to optimize the photocatalytic activity. The excellent photocatalytic degradation efficiency of chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC·HCl) (92.6% within 90 min) and E. coli inactivation efficiency were observed over C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 under visible light irradiation. It is the synergistic effect of p-n heterojunction and modification of C-dots that facilitates the separation and transfer of electron-holes. Meanwhile, the modification of C-dots improves the harvesting of long wavelength solar light of photocatalysts due to its unique up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) characteristics. Eventually, the possible photocatalytic degradation path of the catalyst was inferred by LC-MS spectra, and the degradation mechanism was analyzed. This study sheds light on new possibilities for the application of photocatalysts in various light sources and has broad application prospects in water treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: E. coli, a common bacterial contaminant in water, has become a threat to human health (Zeng et al., 2015; Yi et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018a; Gu et al., 2020). Although antibiotics have emerged as antibacterial agents, such as chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC·HCl), it is constantly released into the environment due to its incomplete degradation of CTC·HCl, resulting in secondary pollution and bacterial resistance (Li et al., 2019; Rtimi et al., 2019). Therefore, there is an urgent need for a more effective technology to simultaneously eliminate bacteria and antibiotics from water (Du et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021a). Traditional methods such as physical, chemical, and biological degradation have been applied to the removal of antibiotics (Liu et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2021a); photocatalytic technology is one of the most promising approaches that can simultaneously inactivate E. coli and degrade pollutants (Dong et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Photocatalytic treatment of water pollutants is a new green technology that utilizes sustainable solar energy (Chen et al., 2010; Zhu et al., 2017; Cao et al., 2021; Senasu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021b). However, most photocatalysts could only be response for UV light and visible light, the near-infrared (NIR) light located in 780–2500 nm is rarely utilized (Huang et al., 2019a; Yuan et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2022). It is of great significance to incorporation carbon quantum dots (C-dots) to broad the light absorption range by up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) characteristics converting NIR light to UV or visible light (Fan et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2018a). Whereas, it inevitably accelerates the fast recombination of charge-separation, hindering the photocatalytic activity and limiting its industriousness applications (Zhang et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021b; Huang et al., 2012). Generally, the p-n heterostructure constructed by two semiconductors have been proved to an effective way to accelerate the separation of photo-inducted carriers due to their energy level regulation (Chen et al., 2018b; Liu et al., 2015; Ren et al., 2019). Cuprous oxide (Cu2O), as a typical p-type semiconductor catalyst, has often been chosen to construct p-n heterojunction owing to the wide range of light absorption. For example, Yu et al. prepared p-Cu2O/n-ZnO heterojunction thin-films for norfloxacin degradation (Yu et al., 2021). Huang et al. prepared a series of p-n heterojunction composite materials (rGO@-Cu2O/BiVO4) that can achieve efficient reduction of Cr (VI) and sulfamethoxazole oxidation under LED light (Huang et al., 2019b). Duan et al. deposited Cu2O on Bi2O3 microplates to form p-n heterojunctions, which enhanced the solar-driven water oxidation activity (Ke et al., 2019). However, the p-n heterojunction may be difficult to maintain the original redox capacity of each component of catalyst, because the holes (or electrons) may reduce the oxidation (or reduction) potential during the transfer process. Accordingly, it is wise to integrate electron mediator with p-n heterojunction in order to simultaneously secure the redox capacity and effective carrier's separation. SrTiO3 is a class of perovskite-structure catalyst with a wide gap (3.20 eV), which could be used as a co-catalyst for photocatalytic degradation (Xu et al., 2011; Jia and IwaseKudo, 2014; Zhou et al., 2021). Considering the energy level structure of SrTiO3 and Cu2O, the Fermi level of p-type Cu2O (p-Cu2O) is close to the edge of the valence band (VB), while that of n-type SrTiO3 (n-SrTiO3) is close to the edge of the conduction band (CB). After the formation of the p-n heterojunction, the Fermi levels of the two semiconductors bend until they reach the equilibrium state. Photogenerated electrons on Cu2O are transferred to SrTiO3, while photogenerated holes are transferred from SrTiO3 to Cu2O. This further facilitates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increases the lifetime of photogenerated carriers (Kong et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2018). Therefore, Cu2O and SrTiO3 should be the most appropriate participants for construction of p-n heterojunction. As for the electron mediator, Pt, Au and Ag were often used to capture electrons on account of the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) property (Lee et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2017a). Nevertheless, it is impracticable for the high cost of materials. In fact, C-dots, as an emerging 0D carbon-based nanomaterial with unique and beneficial optical and electronic properties, such as UCPL characteristics, photo-induced electron transfer properties and size effect, have been applied as an efficient electron mediator in the design of photocatalysts (Mondal et al., 2019; Ming et al., 2012; Le et al., 2020). An eligible photocatalytic material should have perfect redox potentials and visible-light response ability. Therefore, the introduction of C-dots is expected to meet the above requirements.

In this study, we synthesized ternary C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3, a shape similar to that of a chocolate ball covered with sesame seeds. The formation of the C-dots/SrTiO3/Cu2O heterojunction was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The photocatalytic activity of C-dots/SrTiO3/Cu2O was evaluated by degrading antibiotic CTC·HCl and inactivating E. coli. The photodegradation efficiency of CTC·HCl under different pH and various light irradiation were also investigated to speculate the reaction mechanism. In addition, the degradation kinetics and possible degradation pathways were evaluated by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy, UV–visdiffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Finally, the stability and recyclability of the catalyst were evaluated using cyclic degradation experiments. To the best of our knowledge, the construction of an efficient C-dots decorated p-n heterojunction Cu2O/SrTiO3, and its application in the degradation of CTC·HCl and inactivation of E. coli have not been reported to date.

C-dots was synthesized by hydrothermal method as follows: 1.6 g ascorbic acid was dissolved in 15 mL (CH2OH)2 and 25 mL deionized water and stirred magnetically for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 453 K for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained yellow product was dialyzed by dialysis membrane (MW:8000–14000) for 48 h at room temperature. Then, c-dots were obtained and stored in a dry, cool place for subsequent preparation and characterization.

SrTiO3 nanoparticles were synthesized by hydrothermal method: firstly, 3.4 g tetrabutyl titanate and isopropanol (10 mL) were mixed together, and 2.6667 g of strontium chloride hexahydrate was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. The former was added dropwise to the strontium chloride solution under stirring at room temperature. Then, 20 mL 2 M NaOH aqueous solution was added to the mixed solution immediately and stirred until it became emulsion. The suspension was transferred to a Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 473 K for 3 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the precursor was centrifuged, washed with ethanol and deionized water several times, and finally dried in an oven at 333 K. Finally, SrTiO3 was obtained as white power and then ground for subsequent characterization.

The C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 composites were prepared by a two-step method. First, p-Cu2O/n-SrTiO3 heterojunction was synthesized by solution redox method. 0.183 g SrTiO3 was dissolved at 50 mL deionized water, and ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then, CuCl2·2H2O was added under stirring, and 20 mL 1 M aqueous solution of NaOH was dropped immediately, and the mixture was stirred for another 30 min. Subsequently, 1.6 g C6H12O6 was also dropped into above solution under stirring and reacted at 328 K for 3 h. The precipitate was centrifuged, washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, and finally dried at 333 K overnight to obtain p-Cu2O/n-SrTiO3 composites. The synthesis of pure Cu2O was identical to the procedure described above, except SrTiO3 was not added in this case. The molar ratios of Cu2O to SrTiO3 were 0.05, 0.075, 0.10, 0.125, 0.15 and the composites are denoted as CS-5, CS-7.5, CS-10, CS-12.5 and CS-15, respectively. In order to synthesize C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 composites, the obtained C-dots (2, 4, 6, 8 wt %) was added into 50 mL aqueous solution containing 1 mmol CS-12.5 and continuously stirred for 12 h. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 453 K for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the precipitate was washed and finally dried at 333 K. Finally, the obtained C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 composites were marked as 2CCS-12.5, 4CCS-12.5, 6CCS-12.5 and 8CCS-12.5.

E. coli was selected as a representative of common bacteria for photocatalytic antibacterial experiments. The obtained catalysts were used to inactivate E. coli, and the comparative experiments without catalyst or under dark conditions were also carried out. First, the bacterial solution was obtained by culturing in nutrient broth at 310 K for 16 h. After centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min, the obtained bacteria were washed with normal saline solution three times and then diluted with normal saline to yield a cell count of approximately 107 colony forming units (CFU/mL) of E. coli. Then, 10 mg of the catalyst was added to a 50 mL of normal saline solution containing 10 mL of bacteria, and the solution was irradiated for 60 min under visible light irradiation while simultaneously circulating cooling water. 1 mL of bacterial solution was taken every 20 min, and then the concentration of residual bacteria was determined by the plate-counting method. Finally, the culture dish was incubated at 310 K for 24 h.

The catalytic performance of C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 was evaluated by removal of antibiotics CTC·HCl. First, 10 mg of catalyst was added to 50 mL of 15 mg/L CTC·HCl solution. The suspension was stirred continuously in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium on the catalyst surface. The reaction solution was then irradiated with a 500 W Xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm), and 2 mL sample was taken every 15 min. After centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 3 min, the residual catalyst in the solution was removed. The concentration of residual CTC·HCl in the sample was detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900); the λ max wavelength of CTC·HCl is 368 nm. The degradation rate of CTC·HCl was calculated by the following formula:

where C0 is the initial concentration of the CTC·HCl solution (mg/L), and Ct is the concentration of CTC·HCl solution (mg/L) after a certain time of photocatalytic degradation.

To explore the mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction, we carried out free radical capture experiment. Benzoquinone (BQ, 50 mM), isopropanol (IPA, 50 mM) and triethanolamine (TEOA, 50 mM) were used to capture superoxide radicals (O2-), hydroxyl radicals (OH), and h+, respectively. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was used to detect the signals of · O2 -, ·OH; 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) were used as the capture agent.

The photocurrent (PC) response test was carried out on Chi660E electrochemical workstation, and the lamp was turned on/off every 25 s. Mott-Schottky (MS) plots and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves were obtained at a high frequency of 1000 Hz and low frequency of 10 Hz, and the amplitude was set at 0.005 V.

The morphology and lattice structure of Cu2O, SrTiO3, CS-12.5, and 6CCS-12.5 were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. S1a, the as-prepared SrTiO3 was composed of irregular nanoparticles with rough surfaces and diameters in the range of 65–75 nm. In addition, smaller-sized SrTiO3 particles were aggregated together. The morphologies of pure Cu2O (Fig. S1b) mainly consist of polyhedral structure with sharp crystal surface. The particle size distribution inset of Fig. S1b displays that the average size of Cu2O was about 2.2 μm. After polyhedral Cu2O structure was introduced, the SrTiO3 nanoparticles gradually adhered on each crystal surface of Cu2O, and SrTiO3 in CS-12.5 was the most evenly distributed. Fig. S1c shows the morphologies and structure diagrams of CS-12.5. The shape of the catalyst was similar to that of the chocolate ball with sesame on the surface, and its size was approximately 2.16 μm. As shown in SEM and TEM images of Fig. 1 , the morphology and crystal lattice of obtained C-dots and 6CCS-12.5 nanocomposites were further explored. For 6CCS-12.5, tiny C-dots were evenly scattered over the surface of Cu2O and SrTiO3 (Fig. 1a and b). The as-prepared C-dots are spheroidal and well dispersed with a diameter average around 11.5 nm (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM image of 6CCS-12.5 revealed that the lattice spacing of 0.324 nm was ascribed to the (002) plane of C-dots, and 0.3866 nm was attributed to the (100) plane of SrTiO3 (Fig. 1d), which is strong evidence for the formation of C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 heterojunction.

Elemental mapping was used to determine the elemental composition and distribution of 6CCS-12.5. Fig. S2a shows the selected SEM diagram for the mapping analysis. As shown in Figs. S2b–e, Cu, O, Sr, and Ti were uniformly distributed in the polyhedral region, indicating that SrTiO3 was successfully attached to the surface of Cu2O. Moreover, the C element mapping image of 6CCS-12.5 was exhibited in Fig. S2f to prove the prove the distribution of C-dots on the surface of the material. However, due to the trace amount of C element, the mapping image is not as clearly as that of the other elements.

The FTIR spectra of Cu2O, SrTiO3, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 nanocomposites were presented in Fig. 1e. For pristine Cu2O, the band at 620 cm-1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of Cu–O bond. For pristine SrTiO3, the characteristic peaks at 602 cm-1 could be assigned to the vibration of the Ti–O bond; the peak at 1646 cm-1 is ascribed to H–O–H bending vibration of adsorbed water; and the band around 3350 cm-1 were also observed, which could be corresponding to O–H stretching in crystallization water. It could be seen clearly from the image that CS-12.5 nanocomposite combines the characteristic peaks of Cu2O and SrTiO3. In addition, the peaks at 3000-3500 cm-1 broadens while the peak at 1646 cm-1 weakens, which is may be related to the van der Waals forces between the SrTiO3 and Cu2O. Meanwhile, the incorporation of C-dots has not changed the structural characteristics of composites.

The crystal structures of Cu2O, SrTiO3, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 were characterized using XRD. As shown in Fig. 1f, the diffraction peaks at 29.00, 36.51, 42.43, 62.00 and 73.79° correspond to (110), (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystal planes of Cu2O, respectively (JCPDs 77–0199) (Zolfaghari and Zahra, 2018). In addition, the SrTiO3 exhibited five main diffraction peaks at 32.39, 39.95, 46.47, 57.78, 67.82 and 77.2° corresponding to the (110), (111), (200), (211), (220) and (310) crystal planes, respectively (JCPDs 35–0734) (Zhang et al., 2018). The XRD results indicated that CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 composites are predominant constituted by Cu2O and SrTiO3. The XRD patterns of the other catalysts were shown in Fig. S3. Moreover, the diffraction peak at 26° corresponding to carbon is not clearly observed because of its low content and crystalline intensity.

As shown in Fig. 1, the elemental composition and valence of Cu2O, SrTiO3, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 were characterized by XPS. The signals of Ti and Sr were detected in CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 (Fig. 1g). However, the overlapping binding energy between Cu metals and Cu(I) species makes XPS analysis challenging. The peak of Cu 2p in 6CCS-12.5 could be divided into two strong diffraction peaks at 932.4 and 952.5 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the +1 valence of Cu in 6CCS-12.5 (Fig. 1h) (Ma et al., 2015). It is obvious that there are two weak peaks at 944 and 962.5 eV, which are the satellite peaks of Cu 2p. In addition to that, the XPS peaks at 934.6 and 954.5 eV attributed to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 in 6CCS-12.5 are likely related to the presence of a small amount of Cu (II) on the catalyst surface. It is usually observed in Cu2O nanocrystals, for Cu2O is chemically unstable, part of Cu2O is oxidized to CuO. Fig. 1i exhibited the C 1s spectra for CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5. The two peaks centered on 284.65 eV and 286.6 eV, correspond to C–C and C–OH/C–O–C groups respectively (Zhao et al., 2017). Moreover, a characteristic peak centered at 288.4 eV for 6CCS-12.5 is corresponded to CO group due to the introduction of C-dots. It's evidenced that C-dots has stable interactions with O species on the surface of photocatalysts, which keeps sustained reactivity by effectively inhibiting the aggregation and shedding of C-dots. Fig. 1j shows the XPS spectra of O 1s. The two peaks of 529.6 eV and 531.9 eV in CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 indicate different types of oxygen molecules, such as lattice oxygen and absorbed oxygen. The peak at 533.5 eV in 6CCS-12.5 is attributed to CO, due to the participation of C-dots, as shown in the literature (Puleo et al., 2014). The characteristic peaks of Sr 3d at 132.6 and 134.9 eV correspond to Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. S4), indicating that Sr exists in the form of bivalent in SrTiO3, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 (Xia et al., 2018). Similarly, the diffraction peaks of Ti 2p at 458.6 and 465.7 eV correspond to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. S4), indicating that Ti exists as Ti4+ in SrTiO3, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 (Zheng et al., 2017a). The XPS and XRD results together prove the formation of C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 heterojunction, consistently with XRD and TEM.

The antibacterial performance of obtained catalyst was evaluated by inactivating Escherichia coli (E. coli); the Xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) was used as light source. In general, due to contact between bacteria and photocatalysts, the photocatalysts can produce reactive oxygen species under illumination, such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (O2 -), which can directly lead to membrane leakage and finally destroy cell membranes.

In this case, the antibacterial performance of prepared photocatalyst can be evaluated by calculating final colony count of E. coli after the antibacterial experiment. It can be seen from Fig. 2 that the colonies of blank experiment under illumination (Fig. 2a1) are slightly less than the number of colonies under dark conditions (Fig. 2a6), indicating that only illumination is not enough to inactivate E. coli. Bacterial activity was assessed by a live/dead (green/red) bacterial staining experiment. Fig. 2b1 exhibited that almost all the bacteria kept alive and exhibited green spots, suggesting nearly no bacterial inactivation activity of blank experiments. For pristine SrTiO3, the number of colonies have almost no change before and after illumination as shown in Fig. 2a2 and Fig. 2a7 and the antibacterial efficiency was only 10.15%. As shown in Fig. 2b2, after the photocatalytic experiment, only a small fraction of the bacteria was inactivated. For pristine Cu2O, the colonies count gradually declined after illumination (Fig. 2a8) compared to that without illumination (Fig. 2a3), corresponding to the antibacterial efficiency of 39.6%. That's because Cu+ and free radicals play a major role in killing bacteria. Specifically, Cu+ can destroy DNA bacteria, while free radicals are specifically aimed at destroying cell membranes (Fan et al., 2012). Undoubtedly, Fig. 2b3 exhibited red spots, indicating a large portion of bacteria was inactivated. More importantly, the colonies number of 6CCS-12.5 reduced from approximately 107 CFU mL-1 (without illumination) to 103 CFU mL-1 (under illumination) as shown in Fig. 2a4 and Fig. 2a9. The result revealed that 6CCS-12.5 has better antibacterial properties than pure phase Cu2O and the antibacterial efficiency reached 55.7% (Fig. 2b4). After C-dots were introduced, the number of E. coli colonies dropped dramatically and almost all E. coli were inactivated within 60 min (Fig. 2a10) compared with that of without irradiation (Fig. 2a5). In conclusion, 6CCS-12.5 has the highest antibacterial efficiency of 91.58%.

The cell density of E. coli inactivated by different catalysts under visible-light irradiation or in the dark for 60 min are exhibited in Fig. 2b; and the photocatalytic antibacterial efficiencies of Cu2O, SrTiO3, CS-12.5, 6CCS-12.5 and blank experiment under visible-light irradiation for 60 min are shown in Fig. 2c, further confirming the importance of visible-light irradiation and catalysts in photocatalytic antibacterial process. In summary, 6CCS-12.5 exhibited the highest antibacterial efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of p-n heterojunction and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the catalyst was evaluated by the degradation of CTC·HCl (15 mg/L) under visible light irradiation and the results are shown in Fig. 3 , Fig. S5 and Fig. S6. The CS-12.5 exhibited the best degradation efficiency with 40.2% among other catalysts with different amounts of Cu2O, which is obvious higher than Cu2O and SrTiO3 (Fig. 3a). After the participation of C-dots, the degradation rate of CTC·HCl shows significant improvement (Fig. S5), indicating that the addition of C-dots is a feasible method to improve photocatalytic degradation. However, when the incorporation of C-dots exceeds 6 wt %, the degradation efficiency began to decrease, which is speculated to the obstruction of absorption of visible light or the reduction of active sites. It can be observed from Fig. 3a that the degradation efficiency was negligible in the dark condition or in the blank experiment. Significantly, 6CCS-12.5 has the best degradation efficiency of 92.1% after 90 min of irradiation, which is 5.64, 3.69, 2.28 times higher than those of SrTiO3, Cu2O and CS-12.5. The photodegradation curve of CTC·HCl by 6CCS-12.5 under visible light irradiation was shown in Fig. S6a. Obviously, the UV absorption peak shifted, indicating that the structure of the catalyst was destroyed during the degradation process. In addition, the degradation efficiency of 6CCS-12.5 towards CTC·HCl is much higher than that of most reported catalysts (Jiang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2021b; Chen et al., 2017b; Wang et al., 2020) as listed in Table S1.

According to the first-order kinetic equation (Equation (1)), the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of CTC·HCl could be investigated.

where C0 and Ct are the absorbances of the CTC·HCl solution at 0 min and t min, respectively, and kapp (min−1) is the apparent first-order reaction rate constant. Fig. S5 display the kinetics curves of the as-prepared photocatalysts for CTC· HCl degradation. From Fig. 3b, 6CCS-12.5 displays the highest reaction rate constant, which is 15.28, 9.51 and 4.72 times higher than that of SrTiO3, Cu2O and CS-12.5 as shown in Fig. S6 (b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of antibiotic concentration and catalyst dosage
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of initial concentration of CTC·HCl and catalyst dosage on CTC·HCl removal efficiencies by 6CCS-12.5 were also explored. The same dosage of 6CCS-12.5 catalyst was used to degrade 15, 20, 25, 30, and 35 mg/L CTC·HCl solution and the degradation rate decreased from 96.34% to 52% within 90 min (Fig. 3c). Therefore, the optimal reaction concentration for this experiment was determined to be 15 mg/L. Besides, the dosage of catalyst is related to the experimental cost and catalytic efficiency. Dosages of 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5 and 15 mg 6CCS-12.5 catalyst were used to degrade 50 mL of 15 mg/L CTC·HCl solution. As can be seen in Fig. 3c, the degradation rate increases as the amount of catalyst increases from 5 mg to 12.5 mg whereas it decreases if the dosage is up to 15 mg. The decreasing degradation rate is account for that superabundant catalysts blocked the transmission and absorption of incident light, which leads to a lowered photocatalytic effect (Chen et al., 2017a). Therefore, the optimal amount of catalyst in this experiment is 12.5 mg.

In practice, a variety of ions exist in sewage, which must be treated. Therefore, we explored the effects of different cations (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and anions (NO3 -, SO4 2-, H2PO4 -) on the degradation efficiency of CTC·HCl. From Fig. 3d, the degradation efficiency was almost unaffected by the addition of Na+. While the degradation efficiency was much lower after the addition of Mg2+ and Ca2+. This may be because Mg2+ and Ca2+ formed complexes with CTC·HCl and covered the surface of the catalyst, hindering its contact with CTC·HCl and reducing the degradation efficiency (Jin et al., 2007). Next, the same concentration of sodium salt was added to investigate the effect of different anions on the degradation efficiency. As shown in Fig. 3e, the degradation efficiency of CTC·HCl remained nearly unaffected by NO3 -, while SO4 2 - interfered with the degradation effect of CTC·HCl. The addition of H2PO4 - hindered the degradation of CTC·HCl because H2PO4 -, as a chelating agent, adheres to the catalyst surface and reduces the number of active sites (Zheng et al., 2017b).

The removal efficiency of CTC·HCl under different pH conditions was also explored. Simultaneously, we also measured the zeta potential of 6CCS-12.5 under different pH conditions to determine the net charge on the catalyst surface. As shown in Fig. 3f, when the pH was less than 4, the zeta potential of 6CCS-12.5 was positive, indicating a positively charged catalyst surface; when the pH was higher than 4, the zeta potential was negative, and with an increase in pH, the surface negative charges increased. It is well known that CTC·HCl exists in different forms (pKa = 3.3, 7.6 and 9.3), including CTCH3 +, CTCH2 0, CTCH- and CTC2 -, at different pH values (Huang et al., 2017). Therefore, when the solution pH was acidic, the positively charged catalyst cannot fully contact CTCH3 + due to electrostatic repulsion, thus reducing the degradation efficiency. Even when the zeta potential was negative at pH 5, the degradation efficiency was still reduced. We speculated that electrostatic attraction may cause greater coverage of the catalyst surface with CTC·HCl molecules, further reducing the number of active sites. When the pH was alkaline, CTC·HCl existed in the form of CTCH- and CTC2 - and has electrostatic repulsion with the negatively charged catalyst. More importantly, the contact between CTC·HCl and free radical OH- was also reduced under alkaline conditions, leading to the worst degradation efficiency; furthermore, the higher the pH, the worse the efficiency. Therefore, when the solution was neutral (pH = 7), the degradation effect was the best.

The possible intermediates during the photocatalytic degradation towards CTC·HCl were identified by HPLC-MS. The mass spectrometry and the full chromatograms of the samples after different reaction time are shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8. Based on above result, several possible degradation pathways were deduced and shown in Fig. 4 . Owing to their high electron density, functional double bonds (CC), phenolic hydroxyl groups (-OH), chloryl groups (-Cl) and amine groups (-NH2) are vulnerable to attack by free radicals produced by photocatalytic reaction (Wang et al., 2018b). Therefore, these functional groups gradually detach from the CTC·HCl molecule. It can be seen from the mass spectra that the molecular weight gradually separates into lower molecular weight products. First, a molecule with an m/z value of 479 was produced by the hydrolysis of CTC·HCl (Yang et al., 2018). Then, through a series of reactions such as demethylation, removal of water molecules, dichlorination, deamination, and ring-opening reactions, the main intermediates with m/z values of 377, 339, 317, 301, 247, and 232 were obtained. After 90 min of photocatalysis, most of the m/z values were in the range of 100–200; for example, the peaks located at 219, 160, and 174 further decomposed into small, ring-opened molecules that eventually mineralized into carbon dioxide and water. The possible degradation products are listed in Table S2. These results demonstrate that the catalyst can effectively mineralize CTC·HCl molecules under visible-light irradiation, further confirming the TOC results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specific surface area and pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the specific surface area of photocatalyst is an important factor affecting the photocatalytic activity. In Fig. 5 a, N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of Cu2O, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 show the characteristics of type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop. The pore size distribution is exhibited inset of Fig. 5a. The BET specific areas of the photocatalyst are 5.7102 m2/g, 16.261 m (Yi et al., 2018)/g and 35.0291 m2/g, respectively. The results showed that the introduction of STO nanoparticles and 0D c-dots increased the specific surface area of the catalyst to a certain extent, provided more reactive sites for the photocatalytic reaction, and promoted the absorption efficiency obviously, which was consistent with the result in Fig. 3a.

The mineralization ability of a catalyst is an important index for evaluating catalyst performance. Therefore, we tested the removal efficiency of total organic carbon (TOC) during CTC·HCl degradation. As shown in Fig. 5b, the TOC removal efficiencies of 6CCS-12.5 after different time intervals were 30.6, 35.5, 41.06, 49.34, 61.93 and 66%. These results show that 6CCS-12.5 possessed the strongest mineralization ability in the process of CTC·HCl degradation.

The photostability and recyclability of 6CCS-12.5 were examined with four cycles degradation experiments. As shown in Fig. 5c, the degradation efficiency of 6CCS-12.5 decreased slightly after four degradation cycles, which may be due to the loss of catalyst and the influence of light corrosion. These results show that the obtained catalyst is very stable and has potential for practical application. In addition, the crystal phase before and after the reaction showed almost no change (Fig. S9), which further proved that the photocatalyst had good photostability.

The migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers are related to photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5d, under the excitation light at 305 nm, Cu2O and SrTiO3 have the higher emission peak than CS-12.5. Additionally, 6CCS-12.5 exhibits the lowest emission intensity. This result shows that the introduction of C-dots is conducive to the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency.

The charge separation efficiency of catalysts was also examined by transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). From Fig. 5e, it can be clearly seen that the photocurrent intensity of CS-12.5 was significantly higher than Cu2O and SrTiO3. As expected, 6CCS-12.5 present the highest photocurrent, for C-dots is a remarkable electron reservoir to capture photo-induced electron, restraining the recombination of electrons and holes (Guo et al., 2020). Moreover, the EIS Nyquist diagram in Fig. 5f increases the order of 6CCS-12.5< CS-12.5 < Cu2O < SrTiO3, confirming the faster separation of electrons and holes after the introduction of C-dots.

The UV–vis DRS spectra of Cu2O, SrTiO3, CS-12.5 and 6CCS-12.5 were observed to analyze light-absorption capacity, as shown in Fig. 5g. The light absorption of SrTiO3 is located in UV region and Cu2O extend to visible region. The absorption edge of composite CS-12.5 is red-shifted, indicating the introduction of Cu2O greatly promotes the visible-light absorption. It is noteworthy that 6CCS-12.5 has wide light absorption range, covering the UV, visible and even part of near-infrared regions, which is attributed to the UCPL property of C-dots. Eventually, the obtained catalysts proved to have excellent solar energy utilization performance.

The Mott-Schottky plot confirmed the type of semiconductor and the location of the semiconductor band edge. From Fig. 5h and i, Cu2O belongs to a p-type with negative slope, while SrTiO3 is a n-type semiconductor with positive slope. The measured flat band potentials of Cu2O and SrTiO3 were 1.72 eV and −0.97 eV vs. NHE (ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.2), respectively. For n-type semiconductor, the conduction band potential is −0.2 eV lower than the flat band potential, while for p-type semiconductor, the valence band potential is +0.2 eV higher than the flat band potential (Fang et al., 2020); therefore, the EVB value of Cu2O was estimated to be 1.92 eV, and the ECB value of SrTiO3 was estimated to be −1.17 eV. Meanwhile, the band gap of Cu2O and SrTiO3 measured by UV–vis DRS were 2.10 eV and 3.19 eV respectively (inset Fig. 5d). According to the formula Eg = EVB–ECB, the ECB of Cu2O and EVB of SrTiO3 were calculated as −0.18 eV and 2.02 eV, respectively.

To study the mechanism of electron transfer in detail, free-radical capture experiments and ESR tests were carried out. In this process, BQ, IPA and TEOA were used to capture O2 -, ·OH and h+ in the system, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S10, after adding BQ, IPA and TEOA, the degradation efficiencies of CTC·HCl were 42.35, 76.7 and 50.02%, respectively, which indicates that the main active species in the degradation of CTC·HCl by the catalyst was O2 -, followed by h+, and ·OH. ESR experiments were also performed to further determine the production of active species during the degradation process. In Fig. 6b, TEMPO-h+ signal with the peak density of 1:1:1 generates, and the intensity decreases gradually with the irradiation time prolonging from 0 min to 12 min, proving the production of photoinduced h+. Meanwhile, the signals of DMPO-·O2 - and DMPO-·OH with the density of 1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1 appear after 12 min of visible-light irradiation, indicating the production of O2 - and ·OH during the photodegradation process. The above results are the same as the result of the capture experiment.

Additionally, the degradation efficiency for CTC·HCl by 6CCS-12.5 under simulate solar light, UV light, visible light and NIR light were observed to evaluate the light utilization of as-prepared catalysts (Fig. S11). The photodegradation rates of CTC·HCl by 6CCS-12.5 is 1.52, 1.04, 2.3, 5.51 times higher than that of CS-12.5 under simulated solar, UV light, visible light and NIR irradiation. Moreover, 6CCS-12.5 exhibits 59.5% photodegradation rates under NIR light, while the photodegradation rates for CS-12.5 is only 10.8%. Obviously, 6CCS-12.5 is endowed with outstanding NIR light harvesting ability for the UCPL characteristics of C-dots, thus it has excellent degradation efficiency for CTC·HCl.

Summarily, we speculated the possible degradation path of CTC·HCl. Before the two semiconductors were recombined, the Fermi level of the p-type semiconductor Cu2O was close to the top of the valence band, and that of the n-type semiconductor SrTiO3 was close to the bottom of the conduction band as shown in Fig. S12. After the p-n heterojunction was formed, the Fermi level of each semiconductor bent until both were well-aligned. Under visible light irradiation, a large number of photogenerated carriers are generated on the surface of the catalyst. Driven by the internal electric field at the contact interface between the p-semiconductor and n-semiconductor, electrons are transferred from Cu2O to SrTiO3; it's known that C-dots is an efficient electron mediator, so it can transfer the photo-generated electrons and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Simultaneously, the holes on the VB will be transferred from SrTiO3 to Cu2O. The ECB of SrTiO3 was more negative than the standard potential of O2/·O2 - (−0.33 eV), thus some photogenerated electrons on CB can easily reduce the dissolved oxygen on the surface of the material to O2 -. The photogenerated holes on the Cu2O VB cannot oxidize OH- to generate ·OH, because the EVB of Cu2O was more negative than the standard potential of OH-/·OH (+2.4 eV vs. NHE). However, the characteristic peak of DMPO-·OH was observed in the ESR spectra, indicating the presence of ·OH in the photocatalytic process. This may be because O2 - will continue to combine with H+ to generate H2O2, which can be converted to ·OH by reaction with photogenerated electrons; therefore, it also plays a significant role in the catalysis process. Moreover, h+ in the VB can also directly interact with CTC·HCl. In summary, the degradation mechanism of CTC·HCl by C-dots/SrTiO3/Cu2O is displayed in Fig. 7 and the reaction process can be described as follows:
Cu2O + hν →e- + h+
SrTiO3+ hν →e- + h+
O2 + e-→·O2 -
O2 + 2H+ +e- →H2O2
H2O2 + e- →·OH + OH-
(O2 -, h+, OH) + CTC·HCl → Products

In summary, a novel and efficient C-dots/Cu2O/SrTiO3 catalyst have been synthesized and achieved extensive harvesting of solar light and higher photocatalytic efficiency. The composite catalyst 6CCS-12.5 exhibited better photocatalytic degradation efficiency and E. coli inactivation efficiency than Cu2O, SrTiO3 and their composite CS-12.5 because the introduction of C-dots and the formation of p-n heterojunction dramatically promoted photo-induced carriers transfer and effectively inhibited the recombination of photogenerated carriers. More importantly, the UCPL characteristics of C-dots broadens the utilization of solar light for 6CCS-12.5. Finally, the kinetics of the photocatalytic reaction, charge separation mechanism, and possible degradation pathways were also proposed based on experimental results. In a word, this study provides a new strategy for the design of efficient C-dots modified p-n photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic remediation effect towards antibiotics and bacteria.
